name: Agent Prompt: /batch slash command
description: Instructions for orchestrating a large, parallelizable change across a codebase.
audit_details: In Claude Code, this prompt instructs the coordinator model on how to handle the /batch slash command. It decomposes a large codebase change into 5 to 30 independent, self-contained work units sliceable by directory or module, determines a concrete end-to-end test recipe (using tools like claude-in-chrome or verifier scripts), prompts the user if no verification path is found, writes the plan, and spawns isolated background subagents in parallel to execute each unit using isolated worktrees, finally tracking their progress and PR status in a rendered status table.
gemini_rationale: Gemini's 2M token context allows it to keep all subagent coordination, planning, and codebase knowledge in one context window without context fragmentation. However, managing up to 30 parallel subagents is best done by leveraging Gemini's parallel tool calling features (e.g., launching multiple asynchronous background timers or delegating to lightweight subagent nodes) rather than spawning full-blown docker containers. Additionally, the plan compilation phase can be optimized because Gemini can read extensive directories and dependencies simultaneously without losing track of instructions.
gemini_strategy: We integrate the batch orchestration logic into the Antigravity multi-agent orchestration framework. Instead of spawning separate heavyweight Docker sub-instances, the coordinator spawns lightweight Gemini-Flash-powered worker tasks within separated temporary git worktrees. The coordinator tracks progress via asynchronous background task hooks (hooks.on_subagent_complete), and dynamically updates the central execution and PR status table in our workspace walkthrough.md or analysis_results.md artifact.

name: Agent Prompt: /schedule slash command
description: Guides the user through scheduling, updating, listing, or running remote Claude Code agents on cron triggers via the Anthropic cloud API.
audit_details: This system prompt guides the agent in handling the /schedule command. It interacts with the RemoteTrigger API ({action: 'list' | 'get' | 'create' | 'update' | 'run'}) to manage remote agents (routines) running in Anthropic's cloud sandboxed infrastructure. It covers timezone conversion (converting local user timezone to UTC cron expression or RFC3339 timestamp), creating a recurring or one-shot configuration JSON with allowed tools, checking for required MCP connectors, and providing a step-by-step confirmation workflow.
gemini_rationale: The original prompt deals extensively with managing cloud routines via Anthropic's hosted infrastructure and managing connector configurations. In the Gemini/Antigravity architecture, we use local or hybrid background schedulers (such as the schedule background timer/cron tool) that run locally or in secure sandboxed worktrees. The timezone calculations, prompt crafting, and credential validations are still highly relevant, but the remote hosted API calls need to be mapped to our native background scheduling mechanism.
gemini_strategy: We adapt /schedule to work with the Antigravity schedule tool. The agent helps the user craft high-fidelity prompts and schedules (handling timezone conversions automatically). Instead of calling Anthropic's cloud-bound RemoteTrigger API, it schedules one-shot timers or recurring cron jobs using the local Antigravity scheduler which fires off isolated background subagents locally in separate git worktrees, notifying the user when tasks finish or require input.

name: Agent Prompt: /review-pr slash command
description: System prompt for reviewing GitHub pull requests with code analysis.
audit_details: A concise system prompt instructing the agent on how to review a pull request. If no PR number is provided, it lists open PRs using the GitHub CLI (gh pr list). Once a PR number is given, it fetches PR metadata (gh pr view) and diff content (gh pr diff), then performs a thorough code review focused on correctness, conventions, performance, security, and test coverage.
gemini_rationale: Claude Code relies on standard CLI tools like gh for fetch operations. For Gemini, its massive context window can comfortably ingest the entire repository alongside the PR diff, giving it a much deeper understanding of the codebase context (such as adjacent files, types, and interfaces) compared to viewing just the raw diff hunks.
gemini_strategy: We adopt this command by wrapping the GitHub CLI commands in our environment tools. When the user runs /review-pr, Gemini uses gh to retrieve the PR details and diff, and then pulls the complete files of any modified files into its 2M context. The code review is generated with a focus on deep architectural correctness, leveraging in-context definitions rather than guessing from local diff snippets.

name: Agent Prompt: /security-review slash command
description: Comprehensive security review prompt for analyzing code changes with focus on exploitable vulnerabilities.
audit_details: This is a highly detailed, strict prompt for conducting a security review of pending changes. It defines security categories (input validation, auth, crypto, injection, data exposure), strictly filters false positives (using a 17-point 'hard exclusions' list and confidence score rating), and uses a 3-step execution model: 1) identify potential vulnerabilities, 2) launch parallel sub-tasks to filter false positives based on the exclusions list, and 3) filter out anything with confidence under 8.
gemini_rationale: Claude Code uses multiple parallel sub-agents to identify and then double-verify vulnerabilities to prevent false positives and bypass context limitations. With Gemini's massive context, we can perform the 3-step security audit in a single structured session, but the false positive filtering rules and exclusions are absolutely critical to prevent the model from flagging non-exploitable or theoretical warnings.
gemini_strategy: We adapt this into a structured multi-turn cognitive pipeline in our security review skill. Gemini runs a first-pass analysis of the diff hunks and full codebase context to find candidates. Then, instead of spawning sub-agent instances which adds API latency, it runs an internal 'adversarial filtering pass' using its long-context reasoning against the 17 hard exclusions, evaluating each finding on a 1-10 confidence scale. Only findings scoring 8+ are included in the final high-fidelity Markdown report.

name: System Reminder: /btw side question
description: System reminder for /btw slash command side questions without tools.
audit_details: Injected when the user uses /btw to ask a side question. It configures a separate, lightweight agent instance that must answer directly in a single turn without tools, sharing the conversation context but acting as a separate instance. It explicitly forbids the agent from using tools, making promises of action, or referencing that it was interrupted.
gemini_rationale: For Gemini, spawning a completely separate LLM call to handle a side question is an excellent way to prevent breaking the flow of a long-running plan or agent loop. Because the side question agent has no tools, keeping it extremely lightweight and focused solely on in-context information saves significant latency and prevents loop degradation.
gemini_strategy: When the user issues a /btw query, we spawn a zero-tool Gemini Flash agent. We inject this system reminder to restrict its behavior, forcing it to provide a direct, tool-free response based purely on the existing context, while the main agent's autonomous workspace and loop remain completely unaffected.

name: Skill: Schedule recurring cron and run immediately
description: Converts an interval to a cron expression, schedules a recurring task via the cron creation tool, confirms to the user, and immediately executes the task without waiting for the first cron fire.
audit_details: Converts user-defined intervals (e.g. 5m, 1h, 1d) to 5-field cron expressions (rounding to clean intervals if necessary), calls the cron creation tool (CronCreate) to schedule a recurring task, confirms the schedule to the user, and then immediately runs the task in-session without waiting for the first scheduled fire.
gemini_rationale: In Gemini, immediately running the task inside the current warm context before scheduling ensures the user gets immediate feedback on correctness and allows the agent to cache the execution path.
gemini_strategy: We implement this in our background scheduler skill. When a recurring loop or heartbeat task is created, the agent first parses the interval, schedules the recurring job using the Antigravity schedule tool, and then immediately executes the task locally. The output of the run is cached and displayed to the user as a confirmation that the scheduled routine is functional.

name: Skill: Schedule recurring cron and execute immediately (compact)
description: Instructions for creating a recurring cron job, confirming the schedule with the user, and immediately executing the parsed prompt without waiting for the first cron fire.
audit_details: A compact variation of the cron scheduler. It creates a recurring job using the cron tool, confirms the job (including the cadence, the auto-expire timeframe of N days, and how to cancel using the delete tool), and immediately executes the parsed prompt (invoking it via the Skill tool if it is a slash command, or acting on it directly).
gemini_rationale: Compact prompts are ideal for Gemini Flash to avoid instruction dilution. Keeping these instructions concise ensures that the agent follows the scheduling and immediate execution steps precisely without conversational drift.
gemini_strategy: We use this compact skill format in our low-overhead background scheduling routines. The agent calls the schedule tool, prints a standardized confirmation with the job ID and cancellation instructions, and immediately triggers the execution payload, keeping token usage to a minimum.

name: Tool Description: CronCreate
description: Describes the CronCreate tool for enqueuing one-shot or recurring cron-based jobs with jitter and off-minute scheduling guidance.
audit_details: Describes the CronCreate tool, which schedules standard 5-field cron jobs in the user's local timezone. It explains one-shot vs recurring tasks, provides a critical instruction to avoid :00 and :30 minute marks to distribute API load (jitter/off-minute guidance), notes runtime behavior (jobs only fire when REPL is idle, auto-expire after N days), and mentions the use of Monitor for live watching instead.
gemini_rationale: In our architecture, background scheduling is handled by the Antigravity schedule tool (either as a one-shot timer or recurring cron). The off-minute scheduling guidance (avoiding :00 and :30) is a great pattern to keep for load balancing when dealing with cloud-hosted APIs, but since our scheduling runs locally or in a developer-managed background environment, we adapt the tool parameters and limits to match our schedule API.
gemini_strategy: We adapt the CronCreate guidance into the tool description and parameter schema of our Antigravity schedule tool. The agent is instructed to schedule tasks using off-minutes when appropriate, manage standard cron expressions, and output the returned Task ID for cancellation or tracking purposes.

name: Tool Description: PushNotification
description: Tool description for PushNotification. This is a tool that sends a desktop notification in the user's terminal and pushes to their phone if Remote Control is connected.
audit_details: Describes the PushNotification tool, emphasizing the cognitive cost of notifications (pulling user attention). It provides strict heuristics on when not to notify (do not notify for routine progress or quick tasks) and when to notify (long tasks completed, build failed, or blocked on user input). It mandates messages under 200 characters, single-line, and without markdown.
gemini_rationale: Gemini's autonomous execution loops can run for extended periods in the background. Sending notifications must be handled with high precision to prevent developer fatigue. Enforcing a strict 200-character, single-line limitation ensures notifications remain glanceable and action-focused.
gemini_strategy: We integrate these guidelines directly into the tool description of the Antigravity schedule notification prompt or any system-level alert dispatch. The agent is trained to only call for notifications when it has encountered a critical blocking error or completed a multi-step background workflow, adhering to the 200-character constraint.

name: Tool Description: Snooze (delay and reason guidance)
description: Extends the snooze tool description with guidance on choosing delaySeconds relative to the 5-minute prompt cache TTL and writing informative reason fields.
audit_details: Extends the snooze tool description, which schedules resume times in /loop dynamic mode (self-paced tasks). It details how to select delaySeconds to align with the 5-minute Anthropic prompt cache TTL (either under 5 minutes [60s–270s] to keep cache warm, or long heartbeats [1200s–1800s] to pay the cache miss without constant churn, specifically warning against 300s). It also details the sentinel values (<<autonomous-loop-dynamic>>) and requires a descriptive, telemetry-friendly reason field.
gemini_rationale: Gemini's pricing and caching model differ from Anthropic's. For Gemini on Google AI Studio / Vertex AI, context caching (when available) has different TTLs and token limits. However, the concept of keeping the cache warm or using long heartbeats (e.g. 20-30 min) to avoid high API usage for trivial polling is still an excellent cost-control and latency strategy.
gemini_strategy: We adapt this guidance to manage background execution rates in Antigravity. Instead of a snooze tool, we use the schedule one-shot timer (e.g., DurationSeconds=270 for warm polling, or DurationSeconds=1200 for long-running task heartbeats) and enforce a mandatory, telemetry-friendly Prompt parameter that describes the exact action and reason for sleep.

name: System Prompt: Proactive schedule offer after natural future follow-up
description: Instructs the agent to offer a one-line /schedule follow-up after completed work when there is a likely one-time or recurring future action.
audit_details: Instructs the agent to proactively suggest a one-line /schedule command at the end of a completed task if a future follow-up is likely (such as cleaning up a feature flag, checking a staged rollout, removing temporary skips/TODOs, or running recurring sweeps). It outlines signals for one-time vs recurring schedules and instructs the agent to skip it for simple bug fixes/refactors to avoid annoying the user.
gemini_rationale: Gemini is naturally highly conversational and helpful, which sometimes leads it to offer unnecessary options at the end of every message. Ingesting this prompt helps regularize when the agent should proactively suggest background scheduling, keeping the interaction professional and high-value.
gemini_strategy: We incorporate these heuristics into our global prompt post-processing and session closing rules. When Gemini concludes a plan, it evaluates if any 'future obligation' indicators are present (e.g., newly added TODOs or feature flags) and offers a precise, one-line /schedule suggestion only when confidence is high (75%+).

name: System Prompt: Strict proactive schedule offer gate
description: Restricts proactive /schedule offers to completed work with a named future obligation artifact, concrete timing, and no in-session follow-up available.
audit_details: Acts as a strict gate for proactive schedule offers. By default, NO /schedule offer is made. It is only allowed if the current turn left a named artifact with a verbatim future obligation (e.g. a flag cleanup date, skip condition, TODO with date). It forbids offers for unfinished scope or things doable in the current PR, limits it to at most once per session, and mandates a specific phrasing: 'Want me to /schedule … on <date from the artifact>?'
gemini_rationale: A strict gate is highly beneficial for Gemini to prevent it from constantly suggesting scheduling for everything it touches. It keeps the interaction clean and limits API spend.
gemini_strategy: We adapt this strict gate into the coordinator's response formatting rules within the safety and output styling skill. We enforce a strict programmatic check: if Gemini attempts to proactively suggest scheduling, the response must quote a verbatim date or artifact condition from the modified files; otherwise the suggestion is stripped before rendering to the user.

name: Tool Description: TodoWrite
description: Detailed guidelines and real-time task management instructions for creating and updating a structured todo list during complex tasks.
audit_details: Instructs the model on when to proactively use or skip the TodoWrite tool for session-specific task tracking. It requires a structured list for tasks of 3 or more steps or when explicitly requested. It defines states (pending, in_progress, completed) and enforces that exactly one task is in_progress at any time. It mandates dual naming formats: 'content' (imperative) and 'activeForm' (present continuous for spinner display) and forbids marking tasks complete if compiler or test errors exist.
gemini_rationale: Gemini's large context allows it to handle complex state tracking in a single turn, but it can suffer from task drift or neglect state updates if instructions are too prose-heavy. Gemini needs direct execution validation rather than passive status reporting.
gemini_strategy: We integrate this todo tracking natively into the agent's turn execution loop. We automatically maintain the task state in our orchestration wrapper based on the tool calls and compile status, updating progress spinners programmatically. If Gemini attempts to complete a task while compiler or test errors are present, the orchestration loop rejects the state transition.

name: Tool Description: TaskCreate
description: Instructions for initializing and structuring session-specific tasks and their attributes.
audit_details: Provides instructions for creating a structured todo list using TaskCreate, mapping task subjects in imperative form, descriptive requirements, and optional activeForm properties. It details setting up task dependencies (blocks/blockedBy) via TaskUpdate and coordinates these tasks inside plan mode.
gemini_rationale: While subagent-level task lists are useful, Gemini Pro's massive context means we rarely spawn separate subagent tasks for basic exploration. However, standardized task and dependency schemas are crucial for complex, fanned-out parallel subagent execution.
gemini_strategy: We adopt this schema for multi-agent workflows. We wrap task creation and dependency tracking into our execution loop. When Gemini fanned-out agents are running, their dependencies are modeled programmatically in Python, enabling the scheduler to fire unblocked tasks in parallel using Gemini's native parallel tool calls.

name: Tool Description: Workflow
description: Deterministic JavaScript-based orchestration script engine for executing high-scale multi-subagent workflows with concurrency, budgeting, and caching.
audit_details: A major orchestration prompt detailing a Javascript-based workflow engine. It specifies strict literal metadata blocks (meta with name, description, phases) and provides async script hooks: agent() (with options for structured output schemas and git worktree isolation), pipeline() (for concurrent execution without barriers), parallel() (for barrier-based synchronization), log(), phase(), args, budget (token-based limits), and nested workflow() execution. It uses a deterministic runId journal for caching and resuming runs.
gemini_rationale: For simple multi-file searches or checks, executing a JavaScript VM is inefficient. Gemini Pro's 2M token context and native parallel tool calling can collapse multiple separate model runs into a single high-speed turn. However, for massive-scale codebase refactoring or parallel security sweeps, this deterministic flow engine prevents context attention decay and ensures structured execution.
gemini_strategy: We support this workflow model under our Google Antigravity SDK. We translate the Javascript VM helpers (agent, pipeline, parallel) into native Python async routines. We bind the budget helper directly to our active token counters. For task persistence, we implement the runId journaling system to cache successful Gemini turns, skipping duplicate subagent calls during retries.

name: Tool Description: Write (read existing file first)
description: Safety guard requiring the model to read a file first before fully overwriting it.
audit_details: Enforces a strict safety rule: the agent is forbidden from fully overwriting an existing file with the Write tool unless it has already read the file's contents in the current session.
gemini_rationale: Prevents catastrophic data loss and outdated overwrites. Gemini generates code changes extremely fast, but can occasionally hallucinate whole file structures or overwrite local user changes if it doesn't inspect the file beforehand.
gemini_strategy: We enforce this rule at the Python tool execution wrapper layer. Before executing a Write call on an existing path, our safety hook checks the session's read log. If the file has not been read in the current session, the hook blocks the write and returns a system message requiring a prior read.

name: Tool Description: Write
description: Standard rules for writing full files, prohibiting documentation creation and emoji spam unless requested.
audit_details: Instructs the model on using the Write tool to output full files. It directs preferring the Edit tool for partial modifications (minimizing diff token sizes). It strictly prohibits creating markdown/documentation files or using emojis in file outputs unless explicitly requested by the user.
gemini_rationale: Gemini's generation speed can encourage lazy code writing (rewriting full files instead of targeting lines with Edit). This wastes tokens and increases the risk of clobbering concurrent workspace changes.
gemini_strategy: We inject these guidelines into our system prompt and enforce them in our pre-tool hook. If Gemini attempts to call Write on an existing file larger than 50 lines, our supervisor interceptor suggests using the Edit tool instead. We also enforce the documentation and emoji bans.

name: Tool Description: Edit
description: Guidelines for string replacement edits, preserving original indentation, and global string replacements.
audit_details: Instructs on exact string replacements, emphasizing preserving indentation spaces and tabs (ignoring editor prefix line numbers). It commands preferring editing existing files rather than writing new ones, forbids unrequested emojis, and details the replace_all parameter for file-wide string replacements.
gemini_rationale: Gemini is highly sensitive to spacing constraints. Indentation mismatches are common source of syntax errors in Python and YAML configurations. Explicit formatting rules prevent broken files.
gemini_strategy: We adopt this replacement style in our core codebase editing tool. The Python edit helper automatically strips line-number prefixes, performs a strict indentation check, and applies changes. It returns a clean unified diff to Gemini, allowing it to verify the edit instantly.

name: Tool Description: ReadFile
description: Instructions for reading files, images, PDFs, and Jupyter notebooks with size-limit pagination.
audit_details: Details filesystem reading instructions, including pagination limits (MAX_LINES_CONSTANT), absolute path requirements, multimodal parsing of images/screenshots, page-scoped parsing of large PDFs (max 20 pages), and rendering cells and execution outputs from Jupyter notebooks.
gemini_rationale: Because Gemini Pro has a massive 2M context window, we can increase the file reading limits substantially, reducing the need for chunked file reads. Gemini's native multimodal capabilities also make it highly effective at direct PDF and screenshot analysis.
gemini_strategy: We map these rules to our view_file tool. We increase the default read page size limit from 800 to 1500 lines for Gemini, leveraging the large context window. For PDFs and notebooks, our Python helper parses and extracts clean text and cells before injecting them into the Gemini prompt.

name: Tool Description: Grep
description: Ripgrep-based search parameters, regex patterns, glob filters, and multi-line matching instructions.
audit_details: Instructs the model to always use the optimized Grep tool for searches instead of running raw terminal 'grep' or 'rg' commands. It describes regex patterns, glob and file-type filters, output modes (content, files, count), literal braces escaping, and multiline matching flags.
gemini_rationale: Running raw shell search commands can cause hanging processes, timeout errors, and outputs that are difficult for the model to parse. Structured ripgrep search tools return clean, predictable results.
gemini_strategy: We expose the grep_search tool directly to Gemini. The tool runs ripgrep in a controlled subprocess and returns structured JSON output. If Gemini attempts to execute 'grep' or 'rg' in the shell, our command supervisor blocks the command and redirects it to the grep_search tool.

name: Tool Description: LSP
description: Language Server Protocol capabilities, symbol definitions, reference lookups, and call hierarchies.
audit_details: Provides instructions for using the LSP tool to access semantic code intelligence: goToDefinition, findReferences, hover info, document/workspace symbols, goToImplementation, and tracking incoming/outgoing call hierarchies.
gemini_rationale: Language servers are essential for high-accuracy codebase mapping. Although Gemini can extract symbols via broad in-context reasoning, LSP lookups guarantee 100% accuracy and save substantial token context.
gemini_strategy: We hook the LSP tool directly into our active workspace language server process. Gemini can execute multiple semantic intelligence queries in parallel. If no LSP server is configured for a file type, we fall back to robust regex-based Grep searches.

name: Tool Description: REPL
description: JavaScript sandbox REPL for batching multiple async tool calls and running quick single-turn model samplers.
audit_details: Describes a JavaScript programming interface allowing the model to write scripts to loop, branch, and execute async tool calls (Glob, Read, Edit, shell, etc.) in a sealed sandbox VM. It highlights batching all operations into one REPL call to avoid round-trip latencies, using a haiku() sampler, and dynamically registering custom tools.
gemini_rationale: Writing a JavaScript script is highly effective for automating local actions, but Gemini is exceptionally proficient in Python. Replacing the JS REPL with a Python REPL sandbox leverages Gemini's core strength.
gemini_strategy: We replace the JS VM REPL with a sandboxed Python execution REPL. We pre-load the execution context with async client wrappers for all Antigravity tools (e.g. view_file, grep_search, run_command). Gemini is instructed to write a Python script in a single tool turn to handle complex multi-file refactoring tasks.

name: System Prompt: Scratchpad Directory
description: Enforces using a dedicated, session-specific scratchpad directory for all temporary files and scripts.
audit_details: Commands the agent to always use a dedicated, session-specific scratchpad directory for all temporary files, working files, outputs, and intermediate scripts, rather than using /tmp or contaminating the user's project workspace.
gemini_rationale: Critical for workspace hygiene. Preventing the generation of temporary files inside the codebase prevents Git working tree contamination and unnecessary build trigger interruptions.
gemini_strategy: We configure a dedicated scratch folder under our workspace App Data directory (<appDataDir>/brain/<conversation-id>/scratch/). We inject the absolute path of this scratchpad directory into Gemini's system prompt and programmatically direct temporary writes there.

name: System Prompt: Parallel Tool Call Note
description: Guidelines for maximizing efficiency by executing independent tool calls concurrently while keeping dependent calls sequential.
audit_details: Instructs the model to maximize parallel tool calling for independent operations to increase execution speed. It cautions that dependent calls (where input values rely on preceding outputs) must be run sequentially instead.
gemini_rationale: Gemini supports extensive parallel tool execution (often running up to 10 parallel calls). This is a critical speed optimization that reduces turn latency and cuts down round-trip API delays.
gemini_strategy: We place these parallel tool call rules directly in Gemini's core system prompt. The Antigravity executor processes multiple tool calls concurrently using asyncio.gather. We prime the model to execute multiple file reads and directory listings in parallel in its first exploration turn.
